citations.desc: Attending veterinarian and adequate veterinary care (dealers and exhibitors).
citations.narrative:
Two peacocks housed in an outdoor enclosure (Pen 521) were found with feather loss that had not been identified by the
facility prior to this inspection as follows:
• A female peacock was missing most of the feathers from the back side of the base of her neck (approximately 6 inches
long by 2 inches wide) up towards her head.
• A male peacock was missing some feathers from the front portion of the base of his neck such that only downy feathers
were present for an area of about 6 inches wide by 2 inches high.
Failure to promptly identify such conditions delays appropriate assessment and determination of the underlying cause and
may cause undue discomfort to the animals. Daily observation of all animals shall be done to assess their health and well-
being. Timely and accurate information on problems of animal health, behavior, and well-being noted during daily
observations must be communicated to the attending veterinarian.
Corrected at the time of inspection.

Six lovebirds, from an enclosure housing approximately ten birds, were observed outside of their enclosure during
inspection, and per a facility representative, this occurred while an employee was netting to capture and move the birds to
a new enclosure. These six birds escaped from a door that was inadvertently not fully closed. The escaped birds were
first noticed by APHIS officials sitting on the ground near an outdoor enclosure housing Savannah cats. Once noted,
APHIS officials brought the sighting to the attention of the facility, who then initiated a plan to recover the escaped birds.
Upon speaking with the staff member overseeing this area at the time, they stated they were in the process of transferring
four birds from Enclosure #8 to Enclosure #1 in Macaw Alley. Further, they explained that due to multi-tasking while
transferring the birds to Enclosure #1, they hadn’t checked behind them to ensure the door had closed completely,
resulting in six of the birds housed being able to fly out. Animals escaping from primary enclosures are more vulnerable to
harm from surrounding predatory animals, injury, and increased health risks when they are not safely contained or
accounted for. Handling of all animals shall be done as expeditiously and carefully as possible in a manner that does not
cause trauma, overheating, excessive cooling, behavioral stress, physical harm, or unnecessary discomfort.
All six lovebirds were safely recovered during this inspection.

One area of the facility’s perimeter fence did not reach six feet tall. The facility has updated their perimeter fencing to
incorporate the building where the gift shop and other animals are housed. The backside of this building’s patio wall,
serving as a portion of the perimeter fence, measured 57 inches along the right side for approximately 18 feet, where
outdoor seating is available. Having a perimeter fence in place protects the animals in the facility by restricting animals
and unauthorized persons from going through it or under it, permitting contact with the animals in the facility. Additionally,
it serves as a secondary containment system for the animals in the facility. The perimeter fence must be of sufficient
distance from the outside of the primary enclosure to protect nonhuman primates by restricting unauthorized humans, and
animals the size of dogs, skunks, and raccoons, from going through it or under it and having contact with the nonhuman
primates. It must be of sufficient distance to prevent physical contact between animals inside the enclosure and animals
outside the perimeter fence. Nonhuman primates housed in outdoor facilities require a 6-foot-tall perimeter fence around
their enclosure and must be at least 3-feet away from any enclosures or buildings.
Corrected at the time of inspection.

One goat kid had escaped from its primary enclosure, separating it from its mother. The goat was found in a corner of an
adjacent pasture housing ostriches and rheas, lying with two nesting rheas during this inspection. A gap, approximately
four feet long by 10-12 inches high, below the shared fenceline of the goat enclosure was discovered by APHIS officials.
This gap allowed the goat kid to access and enter the bird enclosure. Failure to contain the animals can result in the
inability to access appropriate food and/or water, as well as possibly result in injury and/or death. The outdoor housing
facilities shall be structurally sound and be maintained in good repair to protect the animals from injury and to contain the
animals.
Corrected at the time of inspection.
This is a 1st Re-License Inspection for a Class “C” license. The inspection and exit briefing were conducted with the
applicant.
All items must be in compliance within 2 more inspections or by JUNE 28, 2026, or the applicant will forfeit the license fee
and must wait 6 months to reapply. Please contact your inspector to schedule your next Re-License Inspection.
Regulated activity can continue under your existing USDA license until it expires.
Additional Inspectors:
CARLA THOMAS, VETERINARY MEDICAL OFFICER
KRISTIN NAPOLI, SUPERVISORY ANIMAL CARE SPECIALIST n

narrative:
2.40(b)(3)  Repeat
   
Attending veterinarian and adequate veterinary care (dealers and exhibitors). 
Two peacocks housed in an outdoor enclosure (Pen 521) were found with feather loss that had not been identified by the 
facility prior to this inspection as follows: 
• A female peacock was missing most of the feathers from the back side of the base of her neck (approximately 6 inches

being. Timely and accurate information on problems of animal health, behavior, and well-being noted during daily 
observations must be communicated to the attending veterinarian. 
Corrected at the time of inspection. 
2.131(b)(1) 
   
Handling of animals. 
Six lovebirds, from an enclosure housing approximately ten birds, were observed outside of their enclosure during 
inspection, and per a facility representative, this occurred while an employee was netting to capture and move the birds to 
a new enclosure. These six birds escaped from a door that was inadvertently not fully closed. The escaped birds were

cause trauma, overheating, excessive cooling, behavioral stress, physical harm, or unnecessary discomfort. 
All six lovebirds were safely recovered during this inspection.

3.78(d) 
   
Outdoor housing facilities. 
One area of the facility’s perimeter fence did not reach six feet tall. The facility has updated their perimeter fencing to 
incorporate the building where the gift shop and other animals are housed. The backside of this building’s patio wall, 
serving as a portion of the perimeter fence, measured 57 inches along the right side for approximately 18 feet, where

distance from the outside of the primary enclosure to protect nonhuman primates by restricting unauthorized humans, and

outside the perimeter fence. Nonhuman primates housed in outdoor facilities require a 6-foot-tall perimeter fence around 
their enclosure and must be at least 3-feet away from any enclosures or buildings. 
Corrected at the time of inspection. 
3.125(a)  Repeat
   
Facilities, general. 
One goat kid had escaped from its primary enclosure, separating it from its mother. The goat was found in a corner of an 
adjacent pasture housing ostriches and rheas, lying with two nesting rheas during this inspection. A gap, approximately 
four feet long by 10-12 inches high, below the shared fenceline of the goat enclosure was discovered by APHIS officials. 
This gap allowed the goat kid to access and enter the bird enclosure. Failure to contain the animals can result in the 
inability to access appropriate food and/or water, as well as possibly result in injury and/or death. The outdoor housing 
facilities shall be structurally sound and be maintained in good repair to protect the animals from injury and to contain the 
animals. 
Corrected at the time of inspection. 
 
This is a 1st Re-License Inspection for a Class “C” license. The inspection and exit briefing were conducted with the 
applicant. 
 
All items must be in compliance within 2 more inspections or by JUNE 28, 2026, or the applicant will forfeit the license fee 
and must wait 6 months to reapply. Please contact your inspector to schedule your next Re-License Inspection. 
 
Regulated activity can continue under your existing USDA license until it expires. 
 
Additional Inspectors: 
CARLA THOMAS, VETERINARY MEDICAL OFFICER 
KRISTIN NAPOLI, SUPERVISORY ANIMAL CARE SPECIALIST n
